Long before the sky was mapped, the Moon was a smooth, flawless silver pearl, polished nightly by the Star-Makers. In those ancient days, a great cosmic fox named Vulpis roamed the heavens. Vulpis possessed a coat of burning stardust and an endlessly mischievous heart. He loved chasing blazing comets, but his ultimate prize was the silver pearl that anchored the night sky.

One evening, while the Star-Makers slept, Vulpis leaped from the edge of the Milky Way and caught the Moon in his jaws. He meant to carry it away to his dark den at the edge of the universe. But the Moon was incredibly heavy and bitterly cold. As Vulpis bit down to secure his grip, his sharp teeth cracked the smooth silver surface, leaving deep, jagged marks.

The terrible crunch woke the Star-Makers, who immediately chased the fox away with bright spears of dawn-light. Startled, Vulpis dropped the Moon. It rolled back into its rightful orbit, but the damage was already done. The beautiful, smooth pearl was forever scarred. To this day, the craters on the Moon remain as a reminder of the cosmic fox, capturing the exact shape of Vulpis’s greedy bite.

Long ago, before the mountains had voices, the world was steeped in a heavy, unbroken silence. The sky was ruled by Aethel, the goddess of sound, who kept all noises locked within a silver jar. One day, a curious shepherd named Lior climbed the highest peak, seeking a lost lamb. He found not his sheep, but the sleeping goddess, her silver jar resting carelessly beside her.

Tempted by the faint, muffled melodies vibrating within, Lior reached out and uncorked the vessel. Instantly, a massive cacophony of sound burst forth. The crash of thunder, the whisper of wind, the roaring of oceans, and the lively chatter of humanity. The sudden, overwhelming noise startled Aethel awake. Enraged by the mortal’s audacity, she cursed him without hesitation. "Since you love the sound of the world so much," she decreed, "you shall forever chase it, yet never speak a word of your own."

She transformed Lior into an invisible spirit, binding him tightly to the rocky canyons and deep caves. Now, he can only repeat the last words spoken by others. We now call him Echo, the lonely mountain shepherd, forever answering the distant calls of the world below, but never truly being heard himself.

Myth: Long before the world knew color, the earth and sky were painted only in shades of frost and silver. Aethel, the goddess of light, kept all the vibrant hues of the universe woven tightly into a tremendous tapestry, hidden deep within a glacial cavern in the far north. 

One long winter night, a curious snow fox named Kael wandered into the cavern, seeking shelter from a biting blizzard. Mesmerized by the glowing threads of emerald, violet, and crimson, Kael reached out and tugged at a loose strand. Instantly, the ancient weave began to unravel. Brilliant ribbons of color burst outward, sweeping up through the ice and splashing violently across the dark celestial canvas. 

The sudden explosion of light woke Aethel. At first, she was furious that a mortal creature had undone her masterpiece. However, as she looked up and saw the breathtaking rivers of color dancing against the stars, her anger softened. Instead of cursing Kael, she bound him to the night sky. To this day, the cosmic snow fox races across the frozen heavens, his tail constantly stirring the unraveled threads of Aethel’s tapestry, creating the swirling, painted ribbons we now call the Northern Lights.

Myth: Long before the endless forests knew the cycle of seasons, the trees bore leaves of unending, unbroken emerald. In the heavens above roamed Vulpis, the great cosmic fox whose coat burned with vibrant stardust. When his insatiable greed drove him to bite the Moon, the angry Star-Makers chased him across the cosmos with spears of dawn-light. 

As Vulpis fled, his shimmering tail snagged on the jagged lunar craters. While the finest glowing threads of his fur remained in the sky to form the Northern Lights, the heavier embers of his celestial coat plummeted down to the mortal realm. These burning sparks rained upon the great forests, sinking deep into the roots of the ancient trees. 

The trees did not burn; instead, they absorbed the magnificent cosmic fire. Now, every year, when the great harvest moon rises and Vulpis races closest to the earth's edge, the forests remember the fox’s falling embers. Their leaves ignite in brilliant shades of crimson, copper, and gold. As the delicate, crisp leaves finally flutter down to the forest ground, they perfectly mimic the magnificent rain of scattered stardust from that distant, ancient night, becoming a beautiful, fleeting echo of the mischievous fox’s hasty celestial retreat.

Myth: Long before the sun was born, the world lay trapped in an endless, shivering twilight. Aethel, the Weaver of Dawn, hoarded all warmth and light within a single, radiant sphere of golden thread, locked atop the highest celestial peak. 

Down below, a great frost-wolf named Fenrik watched his pack suffer in the bitter cold. Driven by desperation, Fenrik scaled the obsidian cliffs of the sky while Aethel slumbered. He gently caught the golden sphere in his jaws and fled toward the east. But the sphere was impossibly hot, burning his mouth. As he sprinted across the dark heavens, Fenrik’s grip faltered, and the golden thread began to unspool.

A blazing trail of starlight spilled behind him, scattering glowing embers across the black canvas—creating the sweeping river of stars we now call the Milky Way. When Fenrik finally reached the edge of the world, he could hold the burning sphere no longer. He dropped it, and it shattered against the horizon, spilling a glorious, blinding warmth. 

Aethel awoke, but seeing the frozen world finally bathed in golden light, she let it remain. Thus, the first sunrise was born from the frost-wolf’s brave theft.
